elements.sent-2411-001: We propose a noise schedule that ensures a constant rate of change in the probability distribution of diffused data throughout the diffusion process.
elements.sent-2411-002: To obtain this noise schedule, we measure the rate of change in the probability distribution of the forward process and use it to determine the noise schedule before training diffusion models.
elements.sent-2411-003: The functional form of the noise schedule is automatically determined and tailored to each dataset and type of diffusion model.
elements.sent-2411-004: We evaluate the effectiveness of our noise schedule on unconditional and class-conditional image generation tasks using the LSUN (bedroom/church/cat/horse), ImageNet, and FFHQ datasets.
elements.sent-2411-005: Through extensive experiments, we confirmed that our noise schedule broadly improves the performance of the diffusion models regardless of the dataset, sampler, number of function evaluations, or type of diffusion model.
elements.sent-2411-006: Image generation is one of the most challenging tasks in computer vision, and a variety of deep generative models have been proposed.
elements.sent-2411-007: Generative adversarial networks (GANs) \citep{gan_goodfellow have long been the leading models for high-quality image generation.}
elements.sent-2411-008: These generative models achieved success across a wide range of fields beyond image generation, such as audio \citep{audio1, reverse_noise2 and 3D-point cloud generation \citep{point_cloud1 .}}
elements.sent-2411-009: The performance of generative models is measured using three metrics: sampling speed, sample quality, and mode coverage \citep{diffusion_gans .}
elements.sent-2411-010: Despite the extensive research conducted, satisfying these requirements simultaneously is challenging.
elements.sent-2411-011: For example, GANs have strengths in terms of sampling speed and sample quality, but exhibit a weakness in mode coverage.
elements.sent-2411-012: Denoising diffusion probabilistic models (DDPMs) \citep{nonequiliburium_thermodynamics, ddpm_original are new generative models, that achieve high sample quality and mode coverage.}
elements.sent-2411-013: DDPMs are latent variable models that use a Markov chain to gradually add Gaussian noise to the data, called the forward process.
elements.sent-2411-014: At the end of the forward process, the data are completely destroyed to pure noise.
elements.sent-2411-015: The reverse process is introduced to restore the data from the pure noise by tracing the forward process in the reverse direction.
elements.sent-2411-016: The reverse process uses a Markov chain as well, and a deep neural network is trained to sequentially remove the added noise in latent variables.
elements.sent-2411-017: Although the annealing nature of DDPMs contributes to the high mode coverage, the iterative denoising steps result in slow sampling speed.
elements.sent-2411-018: The number of steps in the reverse process is set to as much as one thousand, as described in \citet{ddpm_original .}
elements.sent-2411-019: In this work, to reduce the number of steps in the reverse process, we propose a noise schedule that ensures a constant rate of change in the probability distribution of diffused data throughout the diffusion process, which we refer to as CRS.
elements.sent-2411-020: As previously stated, the reverse process needs to accurately trace the forward process to restore the data from pure noise.
elements.sent-2411-021: This motivates us to search for noise schedules to enhance the traceability of the forward process.
elements.sent-2411-022: Figure \ref{fig:toy_example depicts a toy example of diffused data distributions with three data points in one-dimensional data space (see Appendix \ref{app:toy_example for details).}}
elements.sent-2411-023: In this example, the diffusion process can be divided into three regions.
elements.sent-2411-024: The first is an area where the probability distributions hardly change, and the noise level can be changed rapidly ($\alpha \lesssim 0.6$).
elements.sent-2411-025: The second is an area where three modes corresponding to the data points emerge, and careful adjustment of the noise level is necessary to enhance mode coverage ($0.6 \lesssim \alpha \lesssim 0.97$).
elements.sent-2411-026: The third is an area where the peaks of each mode become distinct, and the noise level should be changed slowly for sample fidelity ($\alpha \gtrsim 0.97$).
elements.sent-2411-027: As evident from this example, the diffusion process exhibits regions with rapid and slow changes in the probability distributions.
elements.sent-2411-028: To obtain CRS, we measure the rate of change in the probability distribution of the forward process, which we assume represents the traceability.
elements.sent-2411-029: We then determine the noise schedule so that the rate of change remains constant throughout the forward process before training diffusion models.
elements.sent-2411-030: The functional form of CRS is automatically determined for ensuring the constancy of probability-distributional change.
elements.sent-2411-031: Therefore, it is not necessary to pre-define the noise-schedule function, such as linear \citep{ddpm_original and cosine \citep{improved_diffusion .}}
elements.sent-2411-032: CRS is considered a generalized version of \citet{score_matching2 , which determines the noise schedule so that a constant overlap between consecutive probability distributions in the forward process is achieved.}
elements.sent-2411-033: Through extensive experiments, we conclude that CRS broadly improves the performance of image generation in diffusion models.
elements.sent-2411-035: We propose an efficient noise schedule for training and sampling, which we refer to as CRS, to boost the performance of diffusion models.
elements.sent-2411-036: CRS enables us to automatically derive a noise schedule tailored to the target dataset and type of diffusion model.
elements.sent-2411-037: CRS holds significant value as a general framework for customizing noise schedules.
elements.sent-2411-038: Once an appropriate distance metric for measuring the probability-distributional change is defined, the corresponding noise schedule is systematically obtained.
elements.sent-2411-039: We find that the efficient noise schedule depends on both the target dataset and the type of diffusion model.
elements.sent-2411-040: In particular, the efficient noise schedule in latent-space diffusion models is very different from conventionally used ones.
elements.sent-2411-041: Through extensive experiments, we confirm that CRS broadly improves the performance of image generation regardless of the dataset, sampler, number of function evaluations (NFE), or type of diffusion model.
elements.sent-2411-042: The rest of this paper is organized as follows: Section \ref{sec:related_work explains related work on reducing the number of steps in image generation.}
elements.sent-2411-045: Section \ref{sec:experiments presents the experimental results to confirm the superiority of CRS.}
elements.sent-2411-046: Section \ref{sec:discussion presents a discussion, and Section \ref{sec:conclusion concludes this paper.}}

elements.sent-2411-047: Various methods have been proposed to reduce the number of steps in the reverse process.
elements.sent-2411-048: Most methods can be categorized into four strategies: conditional generation, data dimensionality reduction, samplers, and noise schedules.
elements.sent-2411-049: Conditional generation: This strategy reduces the number of steps by eliminating unnecessary sample diversity.
elements.sent-2411-050: The attributes of the samples to be generated are specified through conditioning, such as class and text \citep{adm, conditional1, ldm, conditional2, conditional3, sdxl .}
elements.sent-2411-051: Dimensionality reduction of data: The latent diffusion model (LDM) \citep{ldm, sdxl utilizes a pre-trained autoencoder to reduce the dimensionality of the data, and diffusion models are applied to the reduced data space.}
elements.sent-2411-052: This strategy not only reduces the number of necessary steps but also decreases the processing time per step.
elements.sent-2411-053: Samplers: This strategy devises update rules for latent variables in the reverse process.
elements.sent-2411-054: The Denoising Diffusion Implicit Model (DDIM) \citep{ddim_original introduces a non-Markov chain to generalize DDPMs and proposes a deterministic sampler.}
elements.sent-2411-055: From the perspective of score-based matching, \citet{score_matching1, score_matching2, score_matching3 developed continuous-time diffusion models.}
elements.sent-2411-056: In one of these studies \citep{score_matching3 , the time evolution of latent variables was formulated using a stochastic differential equation (SDE) and an ordinary differential equation (ODE).}
elements.sent-2411-057: They also proposed using general solvers of the SDE and ODE for sampling in diffusion models.
elements.sent-2411-058: Subsequent studies have actively investigated customizing these general solvers for diffusion models \citep{sampler_adaptive_step, sampler_pndm, sampler_dpm_solver, sampler_dpmpp .}
elements.sent-2411-059: Noise schedule: Noise schedules significantly affect the performance of diffusion models \citep{noise_effect .}
elements.sent-2411-060: Many studies have proposed noise schedules \citep{ddpm_original, improved_diffusion, noise_effect , and \citet{shifted_cosine proposed modifying noise schedules depending on the image resolution to be generated.}}
elements.sent-2411-061: However, the functional form in conventional studies, such as linear \citep{ddim_original and cosine \citep{improved_diffusion , is somewhat arbitrary, and it remains unclear whether they are appropriately set.}}
elements.sent-2411-062: The most relevant work to ours is AYS \citep{ays , which determines a noise schedule to minimize the KL divergence between the distributions of real data and generated samples.}
elements.sent-2411-063: Although AYS enables us to obtain noise schedules tailored to the target dataset, sampler, NFE, and trained diffusion model, there are two shortcomings.
elements.sent-2411-064: First, AYS cannot be used as a noise schedule for training because a trained diffusion model is required to optimize noise schedules.
elements.sent-2411-065: Second, AYS is not applicable to several samplers, such as the deterministic sampler proposed in EDM \citep{edm .}
elements.sent-2411-066: In our work, we propose an efficient noise schedule for both training and sampling, which is applicable to any sampler.

elements.sent-2411-086: Similar to the score-based generative model \citep{score_matching1 inspired by simulated annealing \citep{sa_original , our formulation draws inspiration from the adiabatic theorem \citep{adiabatic_theorem in quantum annealing \citep{qa_original .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-087: The adiabatic theorem formulates the traceability of the instantaneous probability distribution (ground state), and a noise schedule is devised on the basis of this traceability \citep{grover_search .}
elements.sent-2411-088: In quantum annealing, it is well known that the noise level must be changed slowly near phase-transition points, where macroscopic statistical quantities undergo significant changes.
elements.sent-2411-089: To ensure a consistent traceability throughout the diffusion process, our criterion to determine the noise schedule is to minimize the maximum distance between consecutive probability distributions in the forward process.
elements.sent-2411-090: Using the distance $D(t, t+\Delta t)$ between the probability distributions $p(\bm{x _{t )$ and $p(\bm{x _{t+\Delta t )$, the aforementioned criterion is expressed as follows: \begin{equation}\min_{\alpha(t)} \left( \max_{t} D(t, t + \Delta t) \right).\end{equation}.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-091: The optimal noise schedule derived from the aforementioned criterion satisfies \begin{equation}D(t, t+\Delta t) = \frac{D(t, t+\Delta t) - D(t, t)}{\Delta t} \Delta t \simeq \left. \frac{\partial D(t, t')}{\partial t'} \right|_{t'=t} \Delta t = \mathrm{const.},\end{equation} where we used $D(t, t)= 0$.

elements.sent-2411-092: Various methods exist for measuring the distance between two probability distributions, such as Kullback-Leibler divergence and Bhattacharyya distance.
elements.sent-2411-093: Note that CRS is applicable to any distance metric.

elements.sent-2411-095: Here, $\alpha (t)$ is the noise-schedule function to be optimized.

elements.sent-2411-182: Through extensive experiments, we confirmed that CRS broadly improves the performance of diffusion models regardless of the dataset, sampler, NFE, or type of diffusion model.
elements.sent-2411-183: However, CRS has room for improvement in the selection of the distance metric.
elements.sent-2411-184: In this work, we adopt FID as the distance metric, which requires a feature model.
elements.sent-2411-185: We acknowledge that the feature models used in this work have the following two issues.
elements.sent-2411-186: The first is that, since the feature models are trained on ImageNet classification, there are concerns about whether they can accurately capture the features of the target datasets.
elements.sent-2411-187: The second is that, as the feature models have not been exposed to diffused images during training, they cannot correctly capture the changes in the probability distributions in the forward process.
elements.sent-2411-188: We expect that self-supervised learning is one of the candidate approaches for obtaining better feature models.
elements.sent-2411-189: Exploration of feature models and other distance metrics remains as future work.
elements.sent-2411-190: By using a more appropriate distance metric, CRS might also be applicable as a training schedule in pixel-space diffusion models.
elements.sent-2411-191: CRS in the latent-space diffusion model significantly differs from conventional noise schedules.
elements.sent-2411-192: The most remarkable difference is the rate of change in $\alpha(t)$ within low-noise regions.
elements.sent-2411-193: In pixel-space diffusion models, a significant number of timesteps are consumed in regions with low noise.
elements.sent-2411-194: It is presumed that, in the pixel-space diffusion models, perceptual expressions are generated in these low-noise regions.
elements.sent-2411-195: On the other hand, in latent-space diffusion models, the autoencoder is responsible for generating perceptual expressions, indicating that there is no need to allocate excessive resources to regions with low-noise levels.
elements.sent-2411-196: We proposed CRS, which ensures a constant rate of change in the probability distribution throughout the diffusion process.
elements.sent-2411-197: CRS does not require a pre-defined functional form of the noise schedule; instead, the functional form tailored to the target dataset and type of diffusion model is systematically derived.
elements.sent-2411-198: In addition, CRS can be applied not only to the sampling schedule combined with arbitrary samplers but also to the training schedule.
elements.sent-2411-199: Through extensive experiments, we confirmed that CRS broadly improves the performance of diffusion models.
elements.sent-2411-200: The most significant challenge with CRS is establishing a method for measuring the distance between probability distributions in the forward process.
elements.sent-2411-201: By identifying a more appropriate distance metric, greater improvements may be achieved.
elements.sent-2411-202: Furthermore, CRS is applicable beyond image generation, such as audio and 3D-point cloud generation, if we can appropriately define the distance.
elements.sent-2411-203: We aim to address this in future work.. \bibliographystyle{iclr2025_conference .}

elements.sent-2411-232: According to \citet{vdmpp , the loss function of the diffusion models has the general form: \begin{equation}L = \frac{1}{2} \mathbb{E}_{t \sim \mathcal{U}(0,1), \varepsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(\bm{0}, \bm{I})} \left[ \tilde{w} \bigl( \lambda(t) \bigr) \cdot - \frac{d \lambda(t)}{dt} \cdot \left\| \tilde{\bm{\varepsilon}}_{\theta} \bigl( \bm{x}(t), \lambda(t) \bigr) - \bm{\varepsilon} \right\|^{2} \right],\end{equation} where $\tilde{w \bigl( \lambda(t) \bigr)$ is the weight for each noise level, and $\lambda (t)$ is the signal-to-noise ratio defined by \begin{equation}\lambda(t) = \log \left( \frac{\alpha(t)^{2}}{\lambda(t)^{2}} \right).\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-233: The aforementioned loss function with $\tilde{w \bigl( \lambda(t) \bigr) = - \bigl( d \lambda(t) / dt \bigr)^{-1}$ is identical to the simplified loss function proposed in \citet{ddpm_original .}}
elements.sent-2411-234: In addition, in EDM \citep{edm , $\tilde{w \bigl( \lambda(t) \bigr)$ and $\lambda (t)$ are set as follows \citep{vdmpp : \begin{equation}\tilde{w} \bigl( \lambda(t) \bigr) = N \bigl( \lambda(t); 2.4, 2.4^{2} \bigr) (e^{-\lambda(t)} + 0.5^{2}), - \frac{d \lambda(t)}{dt} = N \bigl( \lambda(t); 2.4, 2.4^{2} \bigr)^{-1}.\end{equation}.}}}
elements.sent-2411-235: In our experiments, we optimized the following loss function: \begin{equation}L = \frac{1}{2} \mathbb{E}_{t \sim \mathcal{U}(0,1), \varepsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(\bm{0}, \bm{I})} \left[ w \bigl( \alpha(t) \bigr) \left\| \bm{\varepsilon}_{\theta} \bigl( \bm{x}(t), \alpha(t) \bigr) - \bm{\varepsilon} \right\|^{2} \right],\end{equation} where \begin{equation}w \bigl( \alpha(t) \bigr) = \tilde{w} \bigl( \lambda(t) \bigr) \cdot - \frac{d \lambda(t)}{dt},\end{equation} and \begin{equation}\bm{\varepsilon}_{\theta} \bigl( \bm{x}(t), \alpha(t) \bigr) = \tilde{\bm{\varepsilon}}_{\theta} \bigl( \bm{x}(t), \lambda(t) \bigr).\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-035: \begin{equation}L = \frac{1}{2} E_{t \sim U(0,1), \varepsilon \sim N(0, I)} \left[ w \bigl( \alpha(t) \bigr) \left\| \varepsilon_{\theta} \bigl( x(t), \alpha(t) \bigr) - \varepsilon \right\|^{2} \right],\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-236: The training noise schedule and loss weights used in our experiments are summarized in Table \ref{app_tab:loss_function_settings .}
elements.figure-2411-012: \caption{Settings of the loss function.} \label{app_tab:loss_function_settings} \begin{tabular}{lll} setting name training noise schedule: $\alpha(t)$ weighting function: $w(\alpha)$ linear \begin{tabular}{l} linearly interpolate the following points: $\alpha_{0} = 1,$ $\alpha_{1 \leq i \leq 1000} = \sqrt{1 - \tilde{\beta}_{i}} \alpha_{i-1},$ $\tilde{\beta}_{i} = 10^{-4} + \frac{0.02 - 10^{-4}}{999} (i-1).$ \end{tabular} $w(\alpha) = 1$ cosine \begin{tabular}{l} $\alpha(t) = \frac{f(t)}{f(0)},$ $f(t) = \cos \left( \frac{t + 0.008}{1.008} \frac{\pi t}{2} \right).$ \end{tabular} $w(\alpha) = 1$ EDM \begin{tabular}{l} $\alpha(t) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + e^{- \lambda(t)}}},$ $\lambda(t) = - F^{-1}_{\mathcal{N}}(t; 2.4, 2.4^{2}),$ where $F^{-1}_{\mathcal{N}}$ is the cumulative density function of the Normal distribution. \end{tabular} $w(\alpha) = \alpha^{-2} - 0.75$ CRS \begin{tabular}{l} $\frac{d \alpha}{dt} \propto v(\alpha)^{-\xi},$ where $\xi$ is set to 1. \end{tabular} $w(\alpha) = 1$ \end{tabular}
elements.sent-2411-237: Noise schedules for sampling evaluated in our work are summarized in Table \ref{app_tab:noise_schedules_for_sampling .}
elements.sent-2411-239: A common configuration ($\alpha_{\min =0.01, \xi=1.0, R=1.0$) is used across all combinations of datasets, samplers, and NFEs in the latent-space diffusion models, except for $\alpha_{\min}$ on ImageNet 256 $\times$ 256.}
elements.sent-2411-240: Similarly, a common configuration ($\alpha_{\min =0.01, \xi=1.2, R=0.1$) is used in the pixel-space diffusion models.}
elements.sent-2411-241: On the other hand, the hyperparameters are optimized for each combination of sampler and NFE in EDM, as is done in AYS \citep{ays .}
elements.figure-2411-013: \caption{CRS for sampling.} \label{app_tab:noise_schedules_for_sampling} \begin{tabular}{ll} schedule name noise schedule for sampling: $ \left\{ \alpha_{i} | i = 0, 1, ..., T \right\}$ linear \begin{tabular}{l} $\alpha_{i} = \alpha \left( \frac{i}{T} \right),$ where $\alpha(t)$ is the noise schedule for training. \end{tabular} cosine \begin{tabular}{l} $\alpha_{0} = 1,$ $\alpha_{i+1} = \max \left( \sqrt{10^{-3}} \alpha_{i}, \alpha \left( \frac{i}{T} \right) \right),$ where $\alpha(t)$ is the noise schedule for training. \end{tabular} shifted cosine \begin{tabular}{l} $\alpha_{i} = \alpha \left( \frac{i}{T} \right),$ $\alpha(t) = (\alpha_{\max} - \alpha_{\min}) f(t) + \alpha_{\min},$ $f(t) = \cos \left( \frac{\pi t}{2} \right) \frac{r}{\sqrt{1 + (r^{2} - 1) \cos^{2} \left( \frac{\pi t}{2} \right)}},$ $\alpha_{\max} = 1.0, \alpha_{\min} = 0.01, r=\frac{64}{s},$ where $s$ is the resolution. \end{tabular} EDM \begin{tabular}{l} $\alpha_{0} = 1,$ $\alpha_{i \geq 1} = \alpha \left( \frac{i}{T} \right),$ $\alpha(t) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + e^{- \lambda (t)}}},$ $\lambda(t) = \log \frac{1}{\sigma(t)^{2}},$ $\sigma(t) = \left\{ \sigma_{\max}^{\frac{1}{\rho}} + \left( \sigma_{\min}^{\frac{1}{\rho}} - \sigma_{\max}^{\frac{1}{\rho}} \right) (1-t) \right\}^{\rho},$ $\sigma_{\max} = 80, \ \sigma_{\min} = 0.002, \ \rho=7.$ \end{tabular} CRS see Algorithm \ref{app_alg:crs_for_sampling} \end{tabular}
elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{CRS for sampling} \label{app_alg:crs_for_sampling} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \REQUIRE $\alpha_{\min}, R, \xi, T, v(\alpha)$ \ \# T: number of timesteps for sampling \ENSURE $\{ \alpha_{i} | i = 0, 1, ..., T \}$ \STATE $\alpha_{0} \leftarrow 1, \ \alpha_{T} \leftarrow \alpha_{\min}$ \STATE $C \leftarrow \int_{\alpha_{\min}}^{1} v(\alpha)^{\xi} d \alpha$ \STATE obtain $\alpha(t)$ by solving Eq. (\ref{eq:alpha_integral}) \FOR {$i = T$ to $1$} \STATE $\alpha_{\mathrm{cand}} \leftarrow \alpha \left( \frac{i-1}{T} \right)$ \STATE $R_{\mathrm{cand}} \leftarrow \frac{\alpha_{\mathrm{cand}} - \alpha_{i}}{\alpha_{i}}$ \IF{$R_{\mathrm{cand}} < R$} \STATE $\alpha_{i-1} \leftarrow \alpha_{\mathrm{cand}}$ \ELSE \STATE $\alpha_{i-1} \leftarrow (1 + R) \alpha_{i}$ \STATE $C \leftarrow \int_{\alpha_{i-1}}^{1} v(\alpha)^{\xi} d \alpha$ \STATE obtain $\alpha(t)$ by solving Eq. (\ref{eq:alpha_integral}) \ENDIF \ENDFOR \end{algorithmic}

elements.sent-2411-262: A variety of deep generative models have been proposed for image generation, which is one of the most challenging tasks in computer vision field.
elements.sent-2411-263: These models have achieved success across a wide range of fields beyond image generation, such as audio \citep{audio1, reverse_noise2 and 3D-point cloud generation \citep{point_cloud1 .}}
elements.sent-2411-264: The performance of generative models is generally evaluated using three metrics: sampling speed, sample quality, and mode coverage \citep{diffusion_gans .}
elements.sent-2411-265: Despite the extensive research conducted, satisfying these requirements simultaneously remains challenging.
elements.sent-2411-266: Denoising diffusion probabilistic models (DDPMs) \citep{nonequiliburium_thermodynamics, ddpm_original are relatively new generative models that achieve high sample quality and mode coverage.}
elements.sent-2411-267: In this work, to reduce the number of steps in the reverse process, we propose a noise schedule that ensures a constant rate of change in the probability distribution of diffused data throughout the diffusion process, which we refer to as constant rate schedule (CRS).
elements.sent-2411-268: This motivates us to search for noise schedules that can enhance the traceability of the forward process.
elements.sent-2411-269: The first is an area where the probability distributions barely change, and the noise level can be changed rapidly ($\alpha \lesssim 0.6$).
elements.sent-2411-270: The second is an area where three modes corresponding to the data points emerge, and careful adjustment of the noise level is necessary to enhance the mode coverage ($0.6 \lesssim \alpha \lesssim 0.97$).
elements.sent-2411-271: To obtain CRS, we measure the rate of change in the probability distribution of diffused data by simulating the forward process, which we assume represents the traceability.
elements.sent-2411-272: We then determine the noise schedule so that the rate of change in the probability distribution of diffused data remains constant throughout the diffusion process before training the diffusion models.
elements.sent-2411-273: Therefore, it is not necessary to pre-define the noise-schedule function, such as the linear \citep{ddpm_original or cosine \citep{improved_diffusion .}}
elements.sent-2411-274: CRS is considered a generalized version of \citet{score_matching2 , which determines the noise schedule so that a consistent overlap between consecutive probability distributions in the diffusion process is achieved.}
elements.sent-2411-275: We propose an efficient noise schedule for training and sampling to boost the performance of diffusion models.
elements.sent-2411-276: CRS enables us to automatically derive a noise schedule tailored to the target dataset and type of diffusion model, such as pixel space or latent space.
elements.sent-2411-277: Furthermore, CRS allows for the use of any distance metric for measuring the probability-distributional change of diffused data, enabling the customization of noise schedules through identifying an appropriate distance metric.

elements.sent-2411-280: Section \ref{sec:conclusion provides a discussion and conclusion.}

elements.sent-2411-298: To ensure a consistent traceability throughout the diffusion process, our criterion to determine the noise schedule is to minimize the maximum distance between consecutive probability distributions in the diffusion process.
elements.sent-2411-299: Using the distance $D(t, t+\Delta t)$ between the probability distributions of diffused data $p(\bm{x _{t )$ and $p(\bm{x _{t+\Delta t )$, the aforementioned criterion is expressed as \begin{equation}\underset{\alpha(t)} {\operatorname{argmin}} \left( \max_{t} D(t, t + \Delta t) \right).\end{equation}.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-300: The optimal noise schedule derived from the aforementioned criterion satisfies \begin{equation}D(t, t+\Delta t) = \frac{D(t, t+\Delta t) - D(t, t)}{\Delta t} \Delta t \simeq \left. \frac{\partial D(t, t')}{\partial t'} \right|_{t'=t} \Delta t = const.,\end{equation} where we used $D(t, t)= 0$.

elements.algorithm-2411-002: \caption{$v(\alpha)$ on the basis of FID} \label{alg:velocity} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \REQUIRE training dataset $\{ \bm{x}^{(n)} \}_{n=0, ..., N-1}$, discretized noise levels $\{ \alpha_{t} \}_{t = 0, ..., T }$, feature model $\bm{\phi}$ \ENSURE $\mathrm{LinearInterp1d}( \{ \alpha_{t} \}_{t=0, ..., T}, \{ v_{t} \}_{t=0, ..., T})$ \STATE \# Compute statistics of feature vectors \FOR {$n=0$ to $N-1$} \STATE $\bm{x}^{(n)}_{0} \leftarrow \bm{x}^{(n)}$ \IF {diffusion process in latent space} \STATE $\bm{x}^{(n)}_{0} \leftarrow \mathrm{autoencoder}(\bm{x}^{(n)}_{0})$ \ENDIF \STATE \STATE \# Simulate the forward process \FOR {$t=1$ to $T$} \STATE $\beta_{t} \leftarrow \frac{\alpha_{t}}{\alpha_{t-1}}, \delta_{t} \leftarrow \sqrt{1 - \beta_{t}^{2}}$ \STATE $x^{(n)}_{t} \leftarrow \beta_{t} \bm{x}^{(n)}_{t-1} + \delta_{t} \times \mathcal{N}(\bm{0}, \bm{I})$ \ENDFOR \ENDFOR \STATE $\bm{\mu}_{t} \leftarrow mean \left( \{ \bm{x}^{(n)}_{t} \}_{n=0, ..., N-1} \right), \ \forall{t} \in \{ 0, ..., T \}$ \STATE $\bm{\Sigma}_{t} \leftarrow cov \left( \{ \bm{x}^{(n)}_{t} \}_{n=0, ..., N-1} \right), \ \forall{t} \in \{ 0, ..., T \}$ \STATE \STATE \# Compute $v(\alpha)$ using $\bm{\mu}_{t}$ and $\bm{\Sigma}_{t}$ \FOR {$t=0$ to $T-1$} \STATE $v_{t} \leftarrow \frac{\mathrm{FID}(\bm{\mu}_{t}, \bm{\Sigma}_{t}, \bm{\mu}_{t+1}, \bm{\Sigma}_{t+1})}{\alpha_{t} - \alpha_{t+1}}$ \ENDFOR \STATE $v_{T} \leftarrow v_{T-1}$ \end{algorithmic}
elements.algorithm-2411-003: \caption{CRS for sampling} \label{alg:crs_for_sampling} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \REQUIRE probability-distributional change in the diffusion process $v(\alpha)$, maximum and minimum value of $\alpha$ for sampling $(\alpha_{\max}, \alpha_{\min})$, hyperparameters of CRS $(R, \xi)$, number of timesteps for sampling $T$ \ENSURE $\{ \alpha_{i} | i = 0, 1, ..., T \}$ \STATE $\alpha_{0} \leftarrow \alpha_{\max}, \ \alpha_{T} \leftarrow \alpha_{\min}$ \STATE $C \leftarrow \int_{\alpha_{\min}}^{\alpha_{\max}} v(\alpha)^{\xi} d \alpha$ \STATE obtain $\alpha(t)$ by solving Eq. (\ref{eq:alpha_integral}) \FOR {$i = T-1$ to $1$} \STATE $\alpha_{\mathrm{cand}} \leftarrow \alpha \left( \frac{i}{T} \right)$ \IF{$\frac{\alpha_{\mathrm{cand}} - \alpha_{i+1}}{\alpha_{i+1}} < R$} \STATE $\alpha_{i} \leftarrow \alpha_{\mathrm{cand}}$ \ELSE \STATE $\alpha_{i} \leftarrow (1 + R) \alpha_{i+1}$ \STATE $C \leftarrow \int_{\alpha_{i}}^{\alpha_{\max}} v(\alpha)^{\xi} d \alpha$ \STATE obtain $\alpha(t)$ by solving Eq. (\ref{eq:alpha_integral}) \ENDIF \ENDFOR \end{algorithmic}

elements.sent-2411-378: In this work, we proposed CRS, which ensures a constant rate of change in the probability distribution of diffused data throughout the diffusion process.
elements.sent-2411-379: CRS can be derived before training diffusion models and used for both the training and sampling schedules.
elements.sent-2411-380: Through extensive experiments, we confirmed that our noise schedule broadly improves the performance of the pixel-space and latent-space diffusion models regardless of the dataset, sampler, and NFE.
elements.sent-2411-381: The most significant challenge with CRS is establishing a method for measuring the distance between probability distributions of diffused data.
elements.sent-2411-382: By identifying a more appropriate distance metric, greater improvements may be achieved, and CRS might also be applicable as a training schedule in the pixel-space diffusion models.
elements.sent-2411-383: Furthermore, CRS is potentially applicable to tasks beyond image generation, such as audio and 3D-point cloud generation, if we can appropriately define the distance metric.
elements.sent-2411-384: We aim to address this in future work.. \bibliographystyle{icml2025 \onecolumn.}

elements.sent-2411-424: We propose a general approach to optimize noise schedules for training and sampling in diffusion models.
elements.sent-2411-425: Our approach optimizes the noise schedules to ensure a constant rate of change in the probability distribution of diffused data throughout the diffusion process.
elements.sent-2411-426: Any distance metric for measuring the probability-distributional change is applicable to our approach, and we introduce three distance metrics.
elements.sent-2411-427: We evaluated the effectiveness of our approach on unconditional and class-conditional image-generation tasks using the LSUN (Horse, Bedroom, Church), ImageNet, FFHQ, and CIFAR10 datasets.
elements.sent-2411-428: Through extensive experiments, we confirmed that our approach broadly improves the performance of pixel-space and latent-space diffusion models regardless of the dataset, sampler, and number of function evaluations ranging from 5 to 250.
elements.sent-2411-429: Notably, by using our approach for optimizing both training and sampling schedules, we achieved a state-of-the-art FID score of 2.03 without sacrificing mode coverage on LSUN Horse 256 $\times$ 256.
elements.sent-2411-430: Diffusion models are a type of probabilistic generative model that have gained significant attention for their expressive ability to generate high-quality samples across various domains, such as image generation \cite{image_generation1, image_generation2, image_generation3 , image super-resolution \cite{image_super_resolution1, image_super_resolution2 , video synthesis \cite{video_generation1, video_generation2 , and audio generation \cite{audio_generation1, audio_generation2 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-431: Diffusion models were originally proposed in the previous study \cite{nonequiliburium_thermodynamics , and denoising diffusion probabilistic models (DDPMs) \cite{ddpm_original demonstrate that diffusion models can achieve state-of-the-art performance in image generation.}}
elements.sent-2411-432: In diffusion models, two diffusion processes are defined using Markov chains, referred to as the forward process and reverse process.
elements.sent-2411-433: In the forward process, data are diffused by gradually adding Gaussian noise, until the probability distribution of diffused data becomes a Gaussian distribution.
elements.sent-2411-434: In the reverse process, data are generated from Gaussian noise by tracing the forward process in the reverse direction.
elements.sent-2411-435: Noise levels added to data at each step of the diffusion processes are hyperparameters and referred to as the noise schedule.
elements.sent-2411-436: We do not necessarily have to use a same noise schedule for training and sampling, and recent studies \cite{edm, vdmpp have shown that using different noise schedules can lead to improved performance.}
elements.sent-2411-437: Although diffusion models tend to perform well in terms of sample fidelity and mode coverage, they face the challenge of longer generation times due to the iterative function evaluations during sampling \cite{diffusion_gans .}
elements.sent-2411-438: To improve the performance of diffusion models, we propose general approach for optimizing training and sampling schedules that ensure a constant rate of change in the probability distribution of diffused data throughout the diffusion process.
elements.sent-2411-439: We call our approach constant rate scheduling (CRS).
elements.sent-2411-440: As previously stated, the reverse process needs to accurately trace the forward process in the reverse direction to restore the data from pure noise.
elements.sent-2411-441: This motivates us to determine noise schedules that can enhance the traceability of the forward process.
elements.sent-2411-442: Figure \ref{fig:toy_example illustrates a toy example of diffused data distributions with three data points in one-dimensional data space (see Appendix \ref{app:toy_example for details).}}
elements.sent-2411-443: Here, $\alpha$ represents the decay rate of data at each step of the diffusion process.
elements.sent-2411-444: We assume that the probability-distributional change of diffused data represents the traceability of the diffusion process and determines the noise schedule to ensure a constant rate of change in the probability distributions.
elements.sent-2411-445: CRS can be considered general from the following perspectives: 1) CRS can optimize both the training and sampling schedules, 2) any distance metric for measuring probability-distributional change is applicable, allowing the introduction of domain specific distance metrics and the use of different noise schedules for training and sampling, 3) any noise schedule can be interpreted as being optimized using CRS with its own distance metric, providing a unified perspective on the design of noise schedules.
elements.sent-2411-446: Our contributions are summarized as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-447: We propose a general approach to optimize noise schedules for both training and sampling.
elements.sent-2411-448: We can use any distance metric for measuring probability-distributional change and introduce three distance metrics.
elements.sent-2411-449: One is specialized for image generation, while the other two are expected to be applicable across a wide range of domains beyond image generation.
elements.sent-2411-450: Through extensive experiments, we confirm that CRS broadly improves the performance of image generation in pixel-space and latent-space diffusion models regardless of the dataset, sampler, and number of function evaluations (NFEs) from 5 to 250.
elements.sent-2411-451: CRS contributes to the performance improvement at both training and sampling, achieving a state-of-the-art Fr\'{e chet inception distance (FID) \cite{fid_score score of 2.03 on LSUN Horse 256 $\times$ 256 dataset.}}

elements.sent-2411-453: Recent studies regarding fast sampling can be mainly categorized into the following three strategies.
elements.sent-2411-454: Samplers: This strategy devises update rules of diffused data in the reverse process.
elements.sent-2411-455: Denoising diffusion implicit models (DDIMs) \cite{ddim_original introduce a non-Markov chain to generalize DDPMs and propose a deterministic sampler.}
elements.sent-2411-456: In the continuous-time diffusion models, the forward and reverse processes are formulated using a stochastic differential equation (SDE) and ordinary differential equation (ODE) \cite{score_matching3 .}
elements.sent-2411-457: Numerous studies have been conducted to develop efficient solvers tailored for the reverse SDE and ODE in diffusion models \cite{sampler_adaptive_step, sampler_pndm, sampler_dpm_solver, sampler_dpmpp, sampler_ipndm, sampler_unipc, sampler_dpm-v3, sampler_amed .}
elements.sent-2411-458: Noise schedules: Noise schedules significantly affect the performance of diffusion models \cite{noise_effect , and many noise schedules have been proposed \cite{ddpm_original, improved_diffusion, noise_effect, shifted_cosine .}}
elements.sent-2411-459: However, the functional form in these studies, such as the linear \cite{ddim_original and cosine \cite{improved_diffusion functions, is determined somewhat arbitrarily.}}
elements.sent-2411-460: Recently, systematic optimization of noise schedules have begun to be actively studied \cite{noise_gits, noise_jys .}
elements.sent-2411-461: The most relevant approach to CRS is AYS \cite{noise_ays , which determines a noise schedule for sampling to minimize the discretization errors when solving the reverse SDE.}
elements.sent-2411-462: However, noise schedules aimed at improving the training efficiency have been largely overlooked compared with the sampling efficiency, though several studies, such as elucidating diffusion models (EDMs) \cite{edm and variational diffusion models++ (VDM++) \cite{vdmpp , have investigated them.}}
elements.sent-2411-463: Distillations: The distillation-based acceleration is one of the most important direction for fast sampling \cite{distill_org1, distill_org2, distill_consistency, distill_dmd, distill_dmd2, distill_s4s .}
elements.sent-2411-464: CRS is expected to provide better teacher models for distillation methods because it can optimize the training schedule as well.

elements.section-2411-018: General Approach for Optimizing Noise Schedule
elements.sent-2411-471: We explain our motivation and present CRS in more detail.
elements.sent-2411-473: In other words, the distributional change between adjacent timesteps must be small enough to be well approximated by a Gaussian distribution.
elements.sent-2411-474: This observation motivates us to minimize the maximum distributional change in the diffusion process for holding the approximation in Eq. (\ref{eq:gaussian_approx ) throughout the diffusion process.}
elements.sent-2411-475: We can understand the importance of the probability-distributional change in the diffusion process from the perspective of noise conditional score networks (NCSNs) \cite{score_matching1, score_matching2, score_matching3 , which are also the origin of diffusion models.}
elements.sent-2411-476: According to the manifold hypothesis, natural data typically lie on a low-dimensional manifold embedded within a high-dimensional data space.
elements.sent-2411-477: Based on this property, there exist two main challenges in generating samples using only the score of the data distribution $\nabla_{\bm{x \log q(\bm{x )$ starting from a random initialization $\bm{x _{\mathrm{rnd }$ \cite{score_matching1 .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-478: First, $\bm{x _{\mathrm{rnd }$ is likely to be located in regions with $q(\bm{x _{\mathrm{rnd ) \ll 1$, where it is difficult to learn the score accurately.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-479: Second, even if the score can be learned correctly, the score tends to be small in the region with $q(\bm{x _{\mathrm{rnd ) \ll 1$, leading to slow mixing.}}}
elements.sent-2411-480: To address these challenges, NCSNs interpolate between the data distribution and a Gaussian distribution, the support of which is the whole space.
elements.sent-2411-481: If the distributional change between adjacent timesteps is sufficiently small during the interpolation process, a sample generated at a certain timestep is likely to fall into a region where the probability density of the adjacent timestep is high.
elements.sent-2411-482: Therefore, by minimizing the maximum distributional change between adjacent timesteps, it becomes possible to generate samples using the reliable score at all timesteps of the interpolation process.
elements.sent-2411-483: As described above, our criterion to optimize noise schedules is to minimize the maximum distance between consecutive probability distributions in the diffusion process.
elements.sent-2411-484: Using the distance $D(t, t+\Delta t)$ between the probability distributions of diffused data $p(\bm{x _{t )$ and $p(\bm{x _{t+\Delta t )$, our criterion is expressed as \begin{equation}\underset{\alpha(t)} {\operatorname{argmin}} \left( \max_{t} D(t, t + \Delta t) \right).\end{equation}.}}}}

elements.sent-2411-490: Quantum annealing is an algorithm for solving combinatorial optimization problems, where the target cost function to be optimized is interpolated with an initial cost function with a trivial optimal solution.
elements.sent-2411-491: The initial state of the optimization is set to the optimal solution of the initial cost function.
elements.sent-2411-492: By tracing the optimal solutions of the instantaneous cost functions during the interpolation, the optimal solution of the target cost function is then obtained.
elements.sent-2411-493: In quantum annealing, accurately tracing the optimal solution of an instantaneous cost function is crucial, and the adiabatic theorem derives the traceability of the optimal solution at each step.

elements.sent-2411-500: Distance metric based on FID: FID is a widely used distance metric to evaluate the sample fidelity in the field of image generation.

elements.sent-2411-601: We proposed a general approach called CRS to optimize noise schedules for training and sampling in diffusion models.
elements.sent-2411-602: Through extensive experiments, we confirmed that CRS broadly improves the performance of pixel-space and latent-space diffusion models regardless of the dataset, sampler, and NFEs ranging from 5 to 250.

elements.algorithm-2411-004: \caption{$v(\alpha)$ on basis of FID} \label{app_alg:compute_vfid} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \REQUIRE training dataset $\{ \bm{x}^{(n)} \}_{n=0, ..., N-1}$, number of timesteps for simulating the forward process $T$, discretized noise levels $\{ \alpha_{t} \}_{t = 0, ..., T }$, feature model $\bm{\phi}$ \ENSURE $\mathrm{LinearInterp1d}( \{ \alpha_{t} \}_{t=0, ..., T}, \{ v_{t} \}_{t=0, ..., T})$ \STATE \# Compute statistics of feature vectors \FOR {$n=0$ to $N-1$} \STATE $\bm{x}^{(n)}_{0} \leftarrow \bm{x}^{(n)}$ \IF {diffusion process in latent space} \STATE $\bm{x}^{(n)}_{0} \leftarrow \mathrm{autoencoder}(\bm{x}^{(n)}_{0})$ \ENDIF \STATE \STATE \# Simulate the forward process \FOR {$t=1$ to $T$} \STATE $\beta_{t} \leftarrow \frac{\alpha_{t}}{\alpha_{t-1}}$ \STATE $\delta_{t} \leftarrow \sqrt{1 - \beta_{t}^{2}}$ \STATE $x^{(n)}_{t} \leftarrow \beta_{t} \bm{x}^{(n)}_{t-1} + \delta_{t} \times \mathcal{N}(\bm{0}, \bm{I})$ \ENDFOR \ENDFOR \STATE $\bm{\mu}_{t} \leftarrow mean \left( \{ \bm{x}^{(n)}_{t} \}_{n=0, ..., N-1} \right), \ \forall{t} \in \{ 0, ..., T \}$ \STATE $\bm{\Sigma}_{t} \leftarrow cov \left( \{ \bm{x}^{(n)}_{t} \}_{n=0, ..., N-1} \right), \ \forall{t} \in \{ 0, ..., T \}$ \STATE \STATE \# Compute $v(\alpha)$ using $\bm{\mu}_{t}$ and $\bm{\Sigma}_{t}$ \FOR {$t=0$ to $T-1$} \STATE $v_{t} \leftarrow \frac{\mathrm{FID}(\bm{\mu}_{t}, \bm{\Sigma}_{t}, \bm{\mu}_{t+1}, \bm{\Sigma}_{t+1})}{\alpha_{t} - \alpha_{t+1}}$ \ENDFOR \STATE $v_{T} \leftarrow v_{T-1}$ \end{algorithmic}

elements.algorithm-2411-005: \caption{$v_{x}(\alpha)$ for optimizing sampling schedule.} \label{app_alg:compute_vx_sampling} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \REQUIRE training dataset $\{ \bm{x}^{(n)} \}_{n=0, ..., N-1}$, trained diffusion model $\bm{\varepsilon}_{\theta}(\bm{x}, \alpha)$, number of timesteps for simulating the forward process $T$, number of samples used for calculating the mean $S$, maximum and minimum values of $\alpha$ $(\alpha_{s}, \alpha_{e})$ \ENSURE $LinearInterp1d( \{ \alpha_{t} \}_{t=0, ..., T}, \{ v_{t} \}_{t=0, ..., T})$ \STATE \# Compute $\mathbb{E}_{\bm{x}_{\alpha}, \bm{x}_{\alpha'}} \left[ \left\| \bm{x}_{\theta}(\bm{x}_{\alpha}, \alpha) - \bm{x}_{\theta}(\bm{x}_{\alpha'}, \alpha') \right\|_{2}^{2} \right]$ \STATE $\overline{D^{2}_{t}} \leftarrow 0, \ \forall{t} \in \{0, 1, ..., T\}$ \STATE $\Delta \alpha \leftarrow \frac{1}{T} (\alpha_{s} - \alpha_{e})$ \FOR {$n$ in $randperm(N)[:S]$} \STATE $\bm{x}_{0} \leftarrow \bm{x}^{(n)}$ \IF {latent-space diffusion model} \STATE $\bm{x}_{0} \leftarrow autoencoder(\bm{x}_{0})$ \ENDIF \STATE $\bm{y}_{0} \leftarrow \bm{x}_{0}$ \STATE \STATE \# Simulate the forward process \FOR {$t=1$ to $T$} \STATE $\alpha_{t} \leftarrow \alpha_{s} - \Delta \alpha t, \ \sigma_{t} \leftarrow \sqrt{1 - \alpha_{t}^{2}}$ \STATE $\beta_{t} \leftarrow \frac{\alpha_{t}}{\alpha_{t-1}}, \ \delta_{t} \leftarrow \sqrt{1 - \beta_{t}^{2}}$ \STATE $\bm{x}_{t} \leftarrow \beta_{t} \bm{x}_{t-1} + \delta_{t} \times \mathcal{N}(\bm{0}, \bm{I})$ \STATE $\bm{y}_{t} \leftarrow \frac{1}{\alpha_{t}} \left( \bm{x}_{t} - \sigma_{t} \bm{\varepsilon}_{\theta}(\bm{x}_{t}, \alpha_{t}) \right)$ \STATE $\overline{D^{2}_{t}} \leftarrow \overline{D^{2}_{t}} + \frac{1}{S} \left\| \bm{y}_{t} - \bm{y}_{t-1} \right\|_{2}^{2}$ \ENDFOR \ENDFOR \STATE \STATE \# Compute $v(\alpha)$ using $\overline{D^{2}_{t}}$ \FOR {$t=1$ to $T$} \STATE $v_{t} \leftarrow \sqrt{ \frac{1}{\Delta \alpha} \overline{D^{2}_{t}} }$ \ENDFOR \STATE $v_{0} \leftarrow v_{1}$ \end{algorithmic}

elements.algorithm-2411-006: \caption{Adaptive noise schedule for training.} \label{app_alg:compute_vx_training} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \REQUIRE training dataset $\{ \bm{x}^{(n)} \}_{n=0, ..., N}$, initial noise schedule $\alpha(t)$, number of bins $B$, decay rate of EMA $e$, maximum and minimum values of $\alpha$ for training schedule $(\alpha_{\max}, \alpha_{\min})$, infinitesimal variation of $\alpha$ for evaluating the derivative $\Delta \alpha$, minimum $\alpha$ for evaluating $v(\alpha)$ $\alpha_{\mathrm{th}}$, hyperparameter of CRS $\xi$ \ENSURE noise prediction model $\bm{\varepsilon}_{\theta}(\bm{x}, \alpha)$ \STATE $\alpha_{b} \leftarrow \alpha_{\mathrm{th}} + (\alpha_{\max} - \alpha_{\mathrm{th}}) \frac{b}{B}, \ \forall{b} \in \{ 0, ..., B \}$ \STATE $\overline{D^{2}_{b}} \leftarrow 1e^{-6}, \ \forall{b} \in \{ 0, ..., B-1 \}$ \FOR {$n$ in $randperm(N)$} \STATE \# Compute loss for training diffusion models \STATE $\bm{x}_{0} \leftarrow \bm{x}^{(n)}, \ t \sim \mathcal{U}(0, 1), \ \bm{\varepsilon} \sim \mathcal{N}(\bm{0}, \bm{I})$ \STATE $\alpha \leftarrow \alpha(t), \ \sigma \leftarrow \sqrt{1 - \alpha^{2}}$ \STATE $\bm{\varepsilon}_{\alpha} \leftarrow \bm{\varepsilon}_{\theta}(\alpha \bm{x}_{0} + \sigma \bm{\varepsilon}, \alpha)$ \STATE $loss \leftarrow \frac{1}{2} \left\| \bm{\varepsilon}_{\alpha} - \bm{\varepsilon} \right\|_{2}^{2}$ \STATE \STATE \# Evaluate $\overline{D^{2}_{x}}$ using EMA (without gradient) \IF {$\alpha \geq \alpha_{\mathrm{th}}$} \STATE $\alpha' \leftarrow \alpha - \Delta \alpha, \ \beta' \leftarrow \frac{\alpha'}{\alpha}, \ \delta' \leftarrow \sqrt{1 - \beta'^{2}}$ \STATE $\bm{x}_{\alpha'} \leftarrow \beta' \bm{x}_{\alpha} + \delta' \times \mathcal{N}(\bm{0}, \bm{I})$ \STATE $\bm{\varepsilon}_{\alpha'} \leftarrow \bm{\varepsilon}_{\theta}(\bm{x}_{\alpha'}, \alpha')$ \STATE $\bm{y}_{\alpha} \leftarrow \frac{1}{\alpha} \left( \bm{x}_{\alpha} - \sigma \bm{\varepsilon}_{\alpha} \right), \ \bm{y}_{\alpha'} \leftarrow \frac{1}{\alpha'} \left( \bm{x}_{\alpha'} - \sigma' \bm{\varepsilon}_{\alpha'} \right)$ \STATE $b \leftarrow \min \left( \left\lfloor \frac{\alpha - \alpha_{\mathrm{th}}}{\alpha_{\max} - \alpha_{\mathrm{th}}} B \right\rfloor, B-1 \right)$ \STATE $\overline{D^{2}_{b}} \leftarrow e \overline{D^{2}_{b}} + (1-e) \left\| \bm{y}_{\alpha} - \bm{y}_{\alpha'} \right\|_{2}^{2}$ \STATE $v_{b} \leftarrow \sqrt{\frac{1}{\Delta \alpha} \overline{D^{2}_{b}}}$ \ENDIF \STATE \STATE \# Update training schedule $\alpha(t)$ \STATE $\mathcal{X} \leftarrow \{ \alpha_{\min}, \alpha_{0}, \alpha_{1}, ..., \alpha_{B} \}$ \STATE $\mathcal{Y} \leftarrow \{ v_{0}, v_{0}, v_{1}, ..., v_{B-1}, v_{B-1} \}$ \STATE $v(\alpha) \leftarrow LinearInterp1d( \mathcal{X}, \mathcal{Y} )$ \STATE Update $\alpha(t)$ and model parameters $\theta$ \ENDFOR \end{algorithmic}
